title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: hetiv provides tools measuring identifying multi-dimensional structural shocks dynamic models models using two complementary IV approaches: Heteroskedasticity-IV (Rigobon 2003, Rigobon Sack 2004, Lewis 2022, Burri Kaufmann, 2026a): exploits higher variance outcome variables policy event days relative control days identify structural shocks without requiring external instruments. Proxy-IV (Mertens Ravn 2013, Stock Watson 2018): uses external instrument (proxy) correlated shock interest uncorrelated shocks. approaches implemented local projection IV estimators following Jordà (2005), directly produce impulse response functions (IRFs) across multiple horizons. Inference based HC robust standard errors (Montiel Olea et al., 2025). package allows multiple shocks endogenous variables. Weak-instrument robust inference implemented via generalised minimum eigenvalue test Lewis Mertens (2025), nests classical Stock-Yogo (2005) test special case. gweaktest() function direct port Matlab files Lewis Mertens (2025). addition, package provides function kfpredict() predicting underlying unobserved shocks based Kalman-filter, following Burri Kaufmann (2026b). vignette illustrates approaches using simulated four-variable VAR two structural shocks.

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: simulate data VAR(2) N=4N = 4 variables, E=2E = 2 event (policy) shocks, R=2R = 2 regular shocks, P=2P = 2 lags T=500T = 500 observations. policy event occurs every 10th period (approximately 10% observations). Shocks drawn standard normal distribution. impact matrix lower-triangular, corresponding identifying assumption Burri Kaufmann (2026a) first shock contemporaneous effect variables, second shock contemporaneous effect first variable. Deterministic weekday patterns added variables 3 4 illustrate role controls. event indicator Ind equals 1 policy event days 0 control days. illustrate proxy-IV approach, construct noisy external proxy two event shocks adding Gaussian noise true shocks. proxy observed policy event days set NA control days. mirrors typical situation high-frequency identification monetary policy shocks, high-frequency surprises observed policy announcement days.
code: library(hetiv)  # Dimensions N  <- 4   # variables E  <- 2   # event shocks R  <- 2   # regular shocks P  <- 2   # VAR lag order H  <- 20  # IRF horizons  # Impact matrix for event shocks (N x E); lower triangular for recursive ID PsiE      <- matrix(0, N, E) PsiE[, 1] <- c(1.0,  0.5,  0.3,  0.2) PsiE[, 2] <- c(0.0,  1.0,  0.4,  0.3)  SigE <- 4  # event shock variance (scalar, applies to all E shocks)  # Impact matrix for regular shocks (N x R) PsiR      <- matrix(0, N, R) PsiR[, 1] <- c( 1.0, -0.3,  0.2,  0.1) PsiR[, 2] <- c( 0.2,  1.0, -0.1,  0.4)  # VAR coefficient matrices at lags 1 and 2 Phi       <- array(0, dim = c(N, N, P)) Phi[,, 1] <- matrix(c( 0.60,  0.05, -0.04,  0.03,                         0.06,  0.40,  0.05, -0.03,                        -0.05,  0.04,  0.50,  0.06,                         0.03, -0.03,  0.05,  0.70), N, N, byrow = TRUE) Phi[,, 2] <- matrix(c( 0.10,  0.03, -0.02,  0.02,                         0.04,  0.10,  0.03, -0.02,                        -0.03,  0.02,  0.10,  0.03,                         0.02, -0.02,  0.03,  0.10), N, N, byrow = TRUE)  # Simulate — seed is set internally by simulatedata() Nobs <- 500 Nbin <- 100 sim  <- simulatedata(   Phi = Phi, SigE = SigE, PsiE = PsiE, PsiR = PsiR,   Nobs = Nobs, Nbin = Nbin, N = N, R = R, E = E,   Nevn = 10, P = P, eDist = 0, seed = 42 )  # Trim burn-in y_data <- sim$y[(Nbin + 1):(Nobs + Nbin), ] Ind    <- as.integer(sim$IndE[(Nbin + 1):(Nobs + Nbin), 1])  # Inject deterministic weekday variation into variables 3 and 4 y_data[, 3] <- y_data[, 3] + 0.5 * seq_len(Nobs) %% 5 y_data[, 4] <- y_data[, 4] + 0.3 * seq_len(Nobs) %% 5 # RNG state after simulatedata(seed = 42) is deterministic, so no extra seed needed e_proxy       <- sim$eE[(Nbin + 1):(Nobs + Nbin), ] e_proxy[, 1]  <- e_proxy[, 1] + rnorm(Nobs, sd = 1) e_proxy[, 2]  <- e_proxy[, 2] + rnorm(Nobs, sd = 1) e_proxy[Ind == 0, ] <- NA

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: First, estimate misspecified model, allows us later illustrate role control variables. misspsecification stems controlling lagged dependent variables (P=0P = 0) adding deterministic controls.
code: res_het <- hetiv(   y       = y_data,   O       = y_data,   Ind     = Ind,   P       = 0,   H       = H,   E       = E,   norm    = 1,   details = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: second model estimate correctly specified model, P=2P = 2 lags weekday dummies. Note weekday dummies absorb deterministic pattern added data.
code: # Weekday dummies: four indicator variables (one left out as reference) X_data <- matrix(0, nrow = Nobs, ncol = 4) for (i in 1:4) X_data[, i] <- as.integer((seq_len(Nobs)) %% 5 == (i - 1))  res_het_X <- hetiv(   y       = y_data,   O       = y_data,   X       = X_data,   Ind     = Ind,   P       = P,   H       = H,   E       = E,   norm    = 1,   details = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: also can use external proxy e_proxy estimate model via proxy-IV. controls lags included second model. , model correctly specified terms controls, identification relies proxy rather heteroskedasticity. Note proxy-IV function still requires Ind input, , instrument missing, Ind = 1, can later still predict unobserved shock using Kalman filter projection kfpredict().
code: res_proxy <- proxyiv(   y         = y_data,   O         = y_data,   X         = X_data,   Z         = e_proxy,   Ind       = Ind,   P         = P,   H         = H,   E         = E,   norm      = 1,   recursive = FALSE,   details   = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: proxies valid, need additional restrictions principle. However, function allows additionally impose zero restrictions. useful situation doubt validity proxies. addition, imposing restriction true, may improve precision estimates.
code: res_proxy_rec <- proxyiv(   y         = y_data,   O         = y_data,   X         = X_data,   Z         = e_proxy,   Ind       = Ind,   P         = P,   H         = H,   E         = E,   norm      = 1,   recursive = TRUE,   details   = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: estimated impact matrix Psi gives contemporaneous responses NN variables structural shock. can compare estimates across four specifications true PsiE. first sight, differences small. see , still differences terms accuracy estimates, predicted shocks, strength instruments. Impact matrix estimates (Psi) shock 1 across different specifications Impact matrix estimates (Psi) shock 2 across different specifications
code: var_labels <- paste0("Variable ", 1:N)  tab_1 <- round(data.frame(True = PsiE[, 1], HET_IV = res_het$Psi[, 1], HET_IV_X = res_het_X$Psi[, 1],                           Proxy_IV = res_proxy$Psi[, 1], Proxy_IV_rec = res_proxy_rec$Psi[, 1]), 2)  knitr::kable(   tab_1,   col.names = c("True", "HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"),   caption   = "Impact matrix estimates (Psi) for shock 1 across different specifications" ) tab_2 <- round(data.frame(True = PsiE[, 2], HET_IV = res_het$Psi[, 2], HET_IV_X = res_het_X$Psi[, 2],                           Proxy_IV = res_proxy$Psi[, 2], Proxy_IV_rec = res_proxy_rec$Psi[, 2]), 2)  knitr::kable(   tab_2,   col.names = c("True", "HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"),   caption   = "Impact matrix estimates (Psi) for shock 2 across different specifications" )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: can assess accuracy estimates, dynamic causal effects, using plotirf(). function plots IRFs shaded confidence bands one estimation approach. confidence levels can chosen freely. , show 90%, 95%, 99% confidence intervals HET-IV estimates controls.
code: plots_het_X <- plotirf(   IRFest = res_het_X$irf,   IRFse  = res_het_X$se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = c(0.90, 0.95, 0.99) )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx   <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_het_X[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(paste0("HET-IV with controls — Shock ", j, " (90%, 95%, 99% CI)"), size = 11)   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: use simulated data, can compare estimates true ones. plot2irf() overlays two sets IRFs. compare HET-IV estimate (blue) theoretical IRF computed known VAR parameters (red). function computeirf() computes theoretical IRF. standard errors true IRF set zero, confidence bands plotted true IRF.
code: irf_true    <- computeirf(PsiE, Phi, H, cum = FALSE)$irf irf_true_se <- array(0, dim = dim(irf_true), dimnames = dimnames(irf_true))  plots_vs_true <- plot2irf(   IRF1   = res_het_X$irf,   IRF1se = res_het_X$se,   IRF2   = irf_true,   IRF2se = irf_true_se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = 0.90 )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx   <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_vs_true[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV with controls (blue) vs True (red) — Shock ", j), size = 11)   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: can also compare accuracy estimates using correct misspecified models. see confidence intervals wider using misspecified model. especially true third fourth variables affected deterministic weekday pattern. misspecified model account pattern, leads uncertainty biased estimates.
code: plots_vs_true <- plot2irf(   IRF1   = res_het_X$irf,   IRF1se = res_het_X$se,   IRF2   = res_het$irf,   IRF2se = res_het$se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = 0.90 )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx   <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_vs_true[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV with controls (blue) vs without controls (red) — Shock ", j), size = 11)   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: can also compare HET-IV estimates Proxy-IV estimates. confidence intervals slightly wider HET-IV approach, expected given proxy highly correlated true shocks. may case proxy noisy therefore weakly correlated endogenous variable. However, point estimates quite similar across two approaches.
code: plots_vs_proxy <- plot2irf(   IRF1   = res_het_X$irf,   IRF1se = res_het_X$se,   IRF2   = res_proxy$irf,   IRF2se = res_proxy$se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = 0.90 )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx   <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_vs_proxy[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV (blue) vs Proxy-IV (red) — Shock ", j), size = 11)   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: kfpredict() recovers structural shocks reduced-form residuals via Kalman filter prediction. requires estimated impact matrix Psi, residual covariance matrices event control days (Sig, SigR), residuals et. assess accuracy prediction comparing true event shocksfrom DGP. Correlation predicted shocks true shocks shock 2 Correlation predicted shocks true shocks shock 2 correlation true shocks proxy lower one due attenuation bias (see Burri Kaufmann, 2026a). correlation prediction even lower use misspecified model. However, use correct model, correlation larger 0.99 (0.94 Shock 2). Proxy-IV controls also well. Note works also almost equally well imposing recursive restriction. expect given restriction true DGP.
code: shocks_het   <- kfpredict(Sig = res_het$Sig,   SigR = res_het$SigR,                            Psi = res_het$Psi,   et   = res_het$et,                            tol = 1e-4, scale = TRUE)  shocks_het_X <- kfpredict(Sig = res_het_X$Sig, SigR = res_het_X$SigR,                            Psi = res_het_X$Psi, et   = res_het_X$et,                            tol = 1e-4, scale = TRUE)  shocks_proxy <- kfpredict(Sig = res_proxy$Sig, SigR = res_proxy$SigR,                            Psi = res_proxy$Psi, et   = res_proxy$et,                            tol = 1e-4, scale = TRUE)  shocks_proxy_rec <- kfpredict(Sig = res_proxy_rec$Sig, SigR = res_proxy_rec$SigR,                            Psi = res_proxy_rec$Psi, et   = res_proxy_rec$et,                            tol = 1e-4, scale = TRUE) true_shocks <- sim$eE[(Nbin + 1):(Nobs + Nbin), ]  cor_df1<- data.frame(     True       = true_shocks[, 1],     Proxy      = e_proxy[, 1],     HET_IV     = shocks_het[, 1],     HET_IV_X   = shocks_het_X[, 1],     Proxy_IV_X = shocks_proxy[, 1],     Proxy_IV_Rec = shocks_proxy_rec[, 1] )  cor_df2<- data.frame(     True       = true_shocks[, 2],     Proxy      = e_proxy[, 2],     HET_IV     = shocks_het[, 2],     HET_IV_X   = shocks_het_X[, 2],     Proxy_IV_X = shocks_proxy[, 2],     Proxy_IV_Rec = shocks_proxy_rec[, 2] )  knitr::kable(     data.frame(round(cor(cor_df1, use = "complete.obs"), 2))[1, ],     col.names = c("True", "Proxy", "HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"),     caption   = paste0("Correlation of predicted shocks with true shocks for shock ", j) ) knitr::kable(     data.frame(round(cor(cor_df2, use = "complete.obs"), 2))[1, ],     col.names = c("True", "Proxy","HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"),     caption   = paste0("Correlation of predicted shocks with true shocks for shock ", j) )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: gweaktest() implements generalised minimum eigenvalue test Lewis Mertens (2025), robust heteroskedasticity serial correlation applicable multiple endogenous regressors multiple instruments. Note classical Stock-Yogo (2005) test applicable homoskedastic case. hetiv() proxyiv() return WeakData element details = TRUE. data frame contains outcome instrument columns form expected gweaktest(), along lagged deterministic controls used estimation. code extracts relevant columns WeakData data frame runs weak instrument test four specifications. results compiled table easy comparison. Weak instrument test results (Lewis-Mertens generalised minimum eigenvalue test) specification without controls affected weak-instrument problem. specification pass weak instrument tests. Note critical values typically much higher common rule thumb Stock Yogo (2005) test, also, higher HAR test univariate case Montiel Olea Pflueger (2013), around 23.
code: # Helper: extract y, Y, X, Z from WeakData and run gweaktest run_weaktest <- function(weakdata, E) {   # y: outcome variable E+1 (not used as endogenous regressor)   y <- weakdata[, paste0("y", E + 1)]   # Y: first E outcome variables (endogenous regressors)   Y <- weakdata[, paste0("y", 1:E)]   # Z: the E instruments   Z <- weakdata[, paste0("Z", 1:E),]   # X: lagged ("o*") and deterministic ("x*") control columns. In any case, add a constant term as well.   # Note that gweaktest() adds a constant term if missing   ctrl  <- startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "o") | startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "x")   X     <- if (any(ctrl)) cbind(weakdata[, ctrl], matrix(1, nrow(weakdata), 1)) else matrix(numeric(0), nrow(weakdata), 0)       gweaktest(y, Y, X, Z, cov_type = "NW") }  specs <- list(   "HET-IV, no controls"                            = res_het$WeakData,   "HET-IV, with controls"                          = res_het_X$WeakData,   "Proxy-IV, with controls"                        = res_proxy$WeakData,   "Proxy-IV, with controls + recursive restriction" = res_proxy_rec$WeakData )  wt_results <- lapply(specs, run_weaktest, E = E) tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(wt_results), function(nm) {   r <- wt_results[[nm]]   data.frame(     Specification = nm,     Statistic     = round(r$gmin_generalized, 2),     LM_CV         = round(r$gmin_generalized_critical_value, 2),     SY_CV         = round(r$stock_yogo_critical_value_nagar, 2),     Strong        = ifelse(r$gmin_generalized > r$gmin_generalized_critical_value,                            "Yes", "No"),     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) }))  knitr::kable(   tab,   col.names = c("Specification", "Statistic", "LM critical value",                 "SY critical value", "Strong instruments?"),   caption   = "Weak instrument test results (Lewis-Mertens generalised minimum eigenvalue test)" )

code: @Manual{,   title = {hetiv: Heteroskedasticity and Proxy-Based IV Methods},   author = {Daniel Kaufmann and Marc Burri and Valentin Grob},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://dankaufmann.github.io/hetiv/}, }

title: hetiv
text: R package measuring identifying structural shocks using heteroskedasticity- proxy-based instrumental variable (IV) methods daily financial market data. Authors: Daniel Kaufmann, Marc Burri, Valentin Grob Note: work progress. Installation use risk.

title: hetiv
text: Full documentation worked example available package website: https://dankaufmann.github.io/hetiv/ introductory vignette — covering data simulation, HET-IV, Proxy-IV, IRF plots, shock extraction, weak instrument testing — : https://dankaufmann.github.io/hetiv/articles/hetiv-introduction.html

title: hetiv
text: can install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("dankaufmann/hetiv")

title: hetiv
code: library(hetiv)  # y:   T x N matrix of outcome variables # O:   T x M matrix of information set variables # X:   T x K matrix of deterministic variables (optional; e.g. constant, trend, dummies) # Ind: event indicator (0 = control day, 1 = policy day, 2 = contaminated)  res <- hetiv(y = y, O = O, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, norm = 1, details = TRUE)  # With a linear time trend X <- matrix(seq_len(nrow(y)), ncol = 1) res <- hetiv(y = y, O = O, X = X, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, norm = 1, details = TRUE)

title: hetiv
code: # Z: T x E matrix of external instruments (proxies)  res <- proxyiv(y = y, O = O, Z = Z, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, norm = 1, details = TRUE)  # With recursive zero restrictions across shock dimensions res <- proxyiv(y = y, O = O, Z = Z, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 2,                norm = 1, recursive = TRUE, details = TRUE)

title: hetiv
code: plots <- plotirf(IRFest = res$irf, IRFse = res$se,                  HTick = 5, Labels = c("Var 1", "Var 2")) cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots)

title: hetiv
code: # y: T x 1 regressand # Y: T x N matrix of endogenous regressors # X: T x Nx matrix of exogenous controls # Z: T x K matrix of instruments (requires K >= N)  wt <- gweaktest(y = y, Y = Y, X = X, Z = Z)  # Inspect test statistics and critical values wt$gmin_generalized                            # generalised min-eigenvalue statistic wt$gmin_generalized_critical_value             # Lewis-Mertens sharp critical value (Stiefel) wt$gmin_generalized_critical_value_simplified  # conservative closed-form bound wt$stock_yogo_test_statistic                   # Stock-Yogo statistic (Nagar approximation) wt$stock_yogo_critical_value_nagar             # Stock-Yogo critical value  # With Newey-West HAR standard errors and custom bias tolerance wt <- gweaktest(y = y, Y = Y, X = X, Z = Z, cov_type = "NW", tau = 0.10)  # hetiv() and proxyiv() return WeakData when details = TRUE, # which contains the outcome and instrument columns needed for gweaktest() res     <- hetiv(y = y, O = O, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, details = TRUE) z_cols  <- grep("^Z", names(res$WeakData)) y_cols  <- grep("^y", names(res$WeakData)) wt      <- gweaktest(y = res$WeakData[, y_cols[1], drop = FALSE],                      Y = res$WeakData[, y_cols[-1], drop = FALSE],                      X = matrix(numeric(0), nrow(res$WeakData), 0),                      Z = res$WeakData[, z_cols, drop = FALSE])

title: hetiv
code: shocks <- kfpredict(Sig = res$Sig, SigR = res$SigR,                     Psi = res$Psi, et = res$et, tol = 1e-10)

title: hetiv
code: sim <- simulatedata(Phi = Phi, SigE = 4, PsiE = PsiE, PsiR = PsiR,                     Nobs = 500, Nbin = 100, N = 2, R = 2, E = 1,                     Nevn = 5, P = 1, eDist = 0, seed = 42)

what: Compute impulse response functions for a VAR(P) — computeirf
title: Compute impulse response functions for a VAR(P) — computeirf
text: Recursively computes IRFs given impact matrix VAR coefficient array. Optionally cumulates responses selected variables.

title: Compute impulse response functions for a VAR(P) — computeirf
code: computeirf(Psi, Phi, H, cum)

title: Compute impulse response functions for a VAR(P) — computeirf
text: Psi Impact matrix, dimension N x R. Phi Array VAR coefficient matrices, dimension N x N x P. H Horizon (number periods) compute IRFs. cum Logical scalar logical vector length N. TRUE variable , IRF cumulated via cumsum. single value recycled variables.

title: Compute impulse response functions for a VAR(P) — computeirf
text: named list component: irf Array impulse responses, dimension H x N x R. Row names labelled 0 H-1.

what: Linearly interpolate gaps in a data frame — filllinear

text: Fills missing values (NA) every column data frame using linear interpolation, maximum gap consecutive missing observations. Gaps longer gap left NA.

title: Linearly interpolate gaps in a data frame — filllinear

title: Linearly interpolate gaps in a data frame — filllinear

title: First-difference transformation — firstdiff
text: Computes first difference numeric vector time series, .e. x_t - x_{t-1}. Gaps dates ignored; difference always taken respect immediately preceding observation.

title: First-difference transformation — firstdiff
code: firstdiff(TS)

title: First-difference transformation — firstdiff

title: First-difference transformation — firstdiff

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweaktest
text: Tests weak instruments multiple endogenous regressors using generalised minimum eigenvalue statistic Lewis Mertens (2025). test robust heteroscedasticity serial correlation nests classical Stock-Yogo (2005) test special case.

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweaktest
code: gweaktest(   y,   Y,   X,   Z,   cov_type = "EHW",   alfa = 0.05,   tau = 0.1,   points = 1000L,   target = "beta",   crit = "abs" )

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweaktest
text: y Regressand (T x 1 numeric vector matrix). Y Endogenous regressors (T x N numeric matrix). X Exogenous regressors (T x Nx numeric matrix). constant column added automatically one absent X zero columns. Z Instruments (T x K numeric matrix). Requires K >= N. cov_type HAR covariance estimator: "EHW" (Eicker-Huber-White, default) "NW" (Newey-West Lazarus et al. (2018) bandwidth). alfa Nominal significance level (default 0.05). tau Bias tolerance: maximum acceptable relative (absolute, see crit) bias 2SLS estimator (default 0.10). points Number random starting points Stiefel manifold optimisation used compute sharp critical value K > N + 1 (default 1000). target Either "beta" (default) test full coefficient vector, positive integer j <= N target single coefficient beta_j. crit Bias criterion: "abs" (absolute bias, default) "rel" (relative bias). "abs" requires error covariance matrix; "rel" .

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweaktest
text: named list following elements: nobs Number complete observations used. beta_2SLS 2SLS point estimate(s). target Description targeted parameter. criterion Bias criterion used ("abs" "rel"). gmin_generalized Generalised minimum eigenvalue test statistic (Lewis-Mertens). gmin_generalized_critical_value Sharp critical value via Stiefel optimisation. gmin_generalized_critical_value_simplified Conservative simplified critical value (closed-form bound). stock_yogo_test_statistic Stock-Yogo test statistic Nagar approximation. stock_yogo_critical_value_nagar Stock-Yogo critical value Nagar approximation.

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweaktest
text: Lazarus, E., Lewis, D. J., Stock, J. H. Watson, M. W. (2018). HAR Inference: Recommendations Practice. Journal Business & Economic Statistics, 36(4), 541–559. Lewis, D. J. Mertens, K. (2025). Robust Test Weak Instruments 2SLS Multiple Endogenous Regressors. Review Economic Studies, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdaf103. Stock, J. H. Yogo, M. (2005). Testing Weak Instruments Linear IV Regression. D. W. K. Andrews J. H. Stock (Eds.), Identification Inference Econometric Models: Essays Honor Thomas Rothenberg, pp. 80–108. Cambridge University Press.

what: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: Estimates impulse response functions (IRFs) using recursive heteroskedasticity-IV identification (Rigobon, 2003; Rigobon Sachs, 2004; Lewis, 2022; Burri Kaufmann, 2026) combined local projections (Jordà, 2005). Identification exploits difference variance policy event days control days construct instruments endogenous variables.

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
code: hetiv(   y,   O,   X = NULL,   Ind,   P,   H,   E = 1,   norm = 1,   interact = FALSE,   cum = FALSE,   Hstep = 1,   details = FALSE )

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: y Numeric matrix stationary outcome variables (T x N). effect first variable dimension normalized unity horizon 0. variables also used construct heteroskedasticity-based instruments recursive ordering identify multiple dimensions. O Numeric matrix information set variables (T x M). May identical y. Included lags 1 P. X Numeric matrix deterministic variables (T x K). May include constant, time trend, seasonal dummies deterministic controls. Included (lags). Ind Integer vector length T, event indicator: 0 Control day (event) 1 Policy day (event) 2 Contaminated control day (excluded estimation) P Integer. Maximum lag order information set. Set 0 lags (regression constant ). H Integer. Maximum horizon (periods) IRFs estimated. E Integer. Number shock dimensions identify via recursive ordering. norm Numeric scalar. Normalize impact response first variable specific value. Set 1 standard unit-effect normalization. interact Logical. TRUE, lagged information set variables interacted event/non-event dummies. cum Logical vector length N. variable y, whether report cumulative impulse response instead level response. one provided, applied impulse responses. Hstep Integer. Step size horizons. > 1, every Hstep-th horizon estimated starting H = 0. details Logical. TRUE, code saves detailed IV results, slower. set FALSE, returns impulse response standard error (e.g. bootstrap)

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: named list following elements: irf Array (H x N x E) estimated impulse responses. se Array (H x N x E) HC0 heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors. IVRes List ivreg model objects, one per horizon, variable, shock dimension. Obs Data frame observation counts: Tp (policy days), Tc (control days), (contaminated days), Tt (total used). Method Character string "Heteroscedasticity-IV". et Data frame OLS residuals event days (used covariance estimation shock extraction). Sig Covariance matrix residuals event days (used shock extraction). SigR Covariance matrix residuals control days, NA unavailable (used shock extraction). Psi Impact matrix (N x E), .e. irf[1, , ]. WeakData Data frame endogenous variables instruments Lewis-Mertens (2025) weak instrument test.

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: Burri, M. D. Kaufmann (2026). Measuring monetary policy shocks. IRENE Working Papers 24-03, IRENE Institute Economic Research, University Neuchâtel. Burri, M. D. Kaufmann (2026). Multiple monetary policy shocks daily data: heteroskedasticity IV approach. IRENE Working Papers 26-06, IRENE Institute Economic Research, University Neuchâtel. Jordà, Ò. (2005). Estimation Inference Impulse Responses Local Projections. American Economic Review, 95(1), 161–182. Lewis, D. J. (2022). Robust Inference Models Identified via Heteroskedasticity. Review Economics Statistics, 104(3), 510–524. Lewis, D. J. Mertens, K. (2025). Robust Test Weak Instruments 2SLS Multiple Endogenous Regressors. Review Economic Studies, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdaf103 Rigobon, R. (2003). Identification Heteroskedasticity. Review Economics Statistics, 85(4), 777–792. Rigobon, R. Sack, B. (2004). impact monetary policy asset prices. Journal Monetary Economics, 51(8), 1553–1575.

what: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: Extracts structural shocks reduced-form residuals using Kalman filter formula. Given impact matrix Psi covariance matrices residuals event control days, function recovers latent shocks observation. Optionally rescales shocks unit variance.

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
code: kfpredict(Sig, SigR, Psi, et, tol, scale = TRUE)

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: Sig Numeric matrix (N x N). Covariance matrix reduced-form residuals policy event days (e.g. et hetiv()). SigR Numeric matrix (N x N). Covariance matrix reduced-form residuals control (non-event) days. Used back implied shock variances scale = TRUE. Psi Numeric matrix (N x E). Impact matrix, .e. contemporaneous responses N variables E structural shocks (e.g. Psi hetiv()). et Numeric matrix data frame (T x N). Reduced-form residuals event days (e.g. et hetiv()). Rows correspond time periods, columns variables. tol Numeric scalar. Tolerance generalized inverse (MASS::ginv()). Clipped sqrt(.Machine$double.eps). scale Logical. TRUE (default), shocks rescaled unit variance using implied shock variances recovered Sig SigR. FALSE, raw Kalman filter projection returned.

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: numeric matrix (T x E) extracted structural shocks, number rows et one column per shock dimension.

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: Burri, M. Kaufmann, D. (2024). Measuring monetary policy shocks. IRENE Working Paper 24-03, IRENE Institute Economic Research, University Neuchâtel.

title: Log-difference transformation — logdiff
text: Computes first log-difference numeric vector time series, .e. log(x_t) - log(x_{t-1}). Gaps dates ignored; difference always taken respect immediately preceding observation.

title: Log-difference transformation — logdiff
code: logdiff(TS)

title: Log-difference transformation — logdiff
text: TS numeric vector time series.

title: Log-difference transformation — logdiff
text: numeric vector length TS, NA first position.

title: Normalize a time series — normalize
text: Standardizes numeric vector zero mean unit standard deviation (z-score normalization), ignoring missing values.

title: Normalize a time series — normalize
code: normalize(x)

title: Normalize a time series — normalize
text: x numeric vector time series.

title: Normalize a time series — normalize
text: numeric vector length x, mean 0 standard deviation 1.

title: Normalize a time series — normalize

what: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
text: Produces panel IRF plots comparing two sets impulse responses side--side. panel shows point estimates 90% confidence bands approaches. Returns list ggplot objects (one per variable-shock combination) can arranged e.g. gridExtra::grid.arrange().

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
code: plot2irf(IRF1, IRF1se, IRF2, IRF2se, HTick, Labels, ci = 0.9)

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
text: IRF1 Array (H x N x E) impulse responses first approach. Dimnames first dimension must horizon labels (numeric). IRF1se Array (H x N x E) standard errors first approach. IRF2 Array (H x N x E) impulse responses second approach. IRF2se Array (H x N x E) standard errors second approach. HTick Integer. Step size x-axis tick marks. Labels Character vector length N. Variable names used panel titles. ci Numeric scalar (0, 1). Confidence level shaded bands. Defaults 0.90 (90% CI).

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
text: list ggplot objects, one per variable-shock combination, ordered shock (outer loop) variable (inner loop).

what: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
text: Produces panel IRF plots single set impulse responses, optional 90% 95% confidence bands. Returns list ggplot objects (one per variable-shock combination) can arranged e.g. cowplot::plot_grid().

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
code: plotirf(IRFest, IRFse = NULL, HTick, Labels, ci = c(0.9, 0.95))

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
text: IRFest Array (H x N x E) impulse response point estimates. Dimnames first dimension must horizon labels (numeric). IRFse Array (H x N x E) standard errors, NULL suppress confidence bands. HTick Integer. Step size x-axis tick marks. Labels Character vector length N. Variable names used panel titles. ci Numeric vector confidence levels (0, 1). One shaded band drawn per level, wider bands rendered lower opacity. Defaults c(0.90, 0.95).

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
text: list ggplot objects, one per variable-shock combination, ordered shock (outer loop) variable (inner loop).

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
text: Produces panel p-value plots across IRF horizons, optional horizontal reference lines user-supplied significance levels. Returns list ggplot objects (one per variable-shock combination) can arranged e.g. cowplot::plot_grid().

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
code: plotpval(pvals, HTick, Labels, sigLevels = c(0.05, 0.1))

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
text: pvals Array (H x N x E) p-values. Dimnames first dimension must horizon labels (numeric). HTick Integer. Step size x-axis tick marks. Labels Character vector length N. Variable names used panel titles. sigLevels Numeric vector significance levels draw horizontal reference lines. first value drawn solid line, second dashed, values dotted. Defaults c(0.05, 0.10).

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
text: list ggplot objects, one per variable-shock combination, ordered shock (outer loop) variable (inner loop).

what: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: Estimates impulse response functions (IRFs) using user-provided external instruments (proxies) combined local projections (Jordà, 2005). proxy variables serve directly instruments endogenous shock variables. Optionally imposes recursive zero restrictions across shock dimensions supports deterministic controls interaction terms, following conventions hetiv().

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
code: proxyiv(   y,   O,   Z,   X = NULL,   Ind,   P,   H,   E = 1,   norm = 1,   cum = FALSE,   Hstep = 1,   recursive = FALSE,   details = FALSE )

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: y Numeric matrix stationary outcome variables (T x N). effect first variable dimension normalized norm horizon 0. variables also used endogenous regressors instrumented columns Z. O Numeric matrix information set variables (T x M). May identical y. Included lags 1 P. Z Numeric matrix external instruments (T x E). Column e used proxy shock dimension e. Missing values control days (Ind == 0) treated contaminated excluded estimation; missing values policy days (Ind == 1) retained observations remain available shock prediction even instrument unobserved. X Numeric matrix deterministic variables (T x K), NULL (default). May include constant, time trend, seasonal dummies. Included -(lags). Ind Integer vector length T, event indicator: 0 Control day (event) 1 Policy day (event) 2 Contaminated control day (excluded estimation) P Integer. Maximum lag order information set. Set 0 lags (regression constant ). H Integer. Maximum horizon (periods) IRFs estimated. E Integer. Number shock dimensions identify. Default 1. norm Numeric scalar. Normalize impact response first variable value. Set 1 standard unit-effect normalization. cum Logical scalar vector length N. TRUE variable , cumulative IRF reported. single value recycled variables. Default FALSE. Hstep Integer. Step size estimated horizons. > 1, every Hstep-th horizon estimated starting H = 1. Default 1. recursive Logical. TRUE, imposes recursive zero restrictions across shock dimensions: shock e > 1, variables instruments dimensions 1, ..., e-1 added controls. Default FALSE. details Logical. TRUE, returns detailed results including IV model objects, OLS residuals, covariance matrices. FALSE (default), returns impulse responses standard errors (faster; use bootstrap).

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: named list. Always contains: irf Array (H x N x E) estimated impulse responses. se Array (H x N x E) HC0 heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors. Method Character string "Proxy-IV". details = TRUE, additionally contains: IVRes List ivreg model objects, one per horizon, variable, shock dimension. OLSRes List OLS model objects used residual-based covariance estimation, one per outcome variable. Obs Data frame observation counts: Tp (policy days), Tc (control days), (contaminated days), Tt (total used). et Data frame OLS residuals event days. Sig Covariance matrix residuals event days. SigR Covariance matrix residuals control days, NA unavailable. Psi Impact matrix (N x E), .e. irf[1, , ]. WeakData Data frame endogenous variables instruments Lewis-Mertens (2025) weak instrument test.

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: Jordà, Ò. (2005). Estimation Inference Impulse Responses Local Projections. American Economic Review, 95(1), 161–182. Lewis, D. J. Mertens, K. (2025). Robust Test Weak Instruments 2SLS Multiple Endogenous Regressors. Review Economic Studies, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdaf103 Mertens, K. Ravn, M. O. (2013). Dynamic Effects Personal Corporate Income Tax Changes United States. American Economic Review, 103(4), 1212–1247. Stock, J. H. Watson, M. W. (2018). Identification Estimation Dynamic Causal Effects Macroeconomics Using External Instruments. Economic Journal, 128(610), 917–948.

what: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
text: Simulates data VAR(P) model two types structural shocks: regular shocks (always present) event shocks (occurring every Nevn periods). shock distribution can normal, Student-t, GARCH(1,1).

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
code: simulatedata(Phi, SigE, PsiE, PsiR, Nobs, Nbin, N, R, E, Nevn, P, eDist, seed)

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
text: Phi Array VAR coefficient matrices, dimension N x N x P. SigE Variance event shocks. applies E shocks. PsiE Impact matrix event shocks, dimension N x E. PsiR Impact matrix regular shocks, dimension N x R. Nobs Number observations retain (burn-). Nbin Number burn-observations discarded start. N Number variables VAR. R Number regular shocks. E Number event shocks. Currently E = 1 supported. Nevn Event frequency: event shock occurs every Nevn periods. Set 0 suppress event shocks entirely (heteroskedasticity). P VAR lag order. eDist Shock distribution. Use 0 standard normal; positive integer Student-t many degrees freedom; numeric vector c(alpha, beta) GARCH(1,1) ARCH effect alpha persistence beta. seed Integer seed passed set.seed() reproducibility. Use NA skip seeding.

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
text: named list components: y Simulated VAR data, dimension (Nobs + Nbin) x N. IndE Event indicator vector, length Nobs + Nbin. eR Simulated regular shocks, dimension (Nobs + Nbin) x R. eE Simulated event shocks, dimension (Nobs + Nbin) x E. e Composite structural shocks, dimension (Nobs + Nbin) x N. Phi VAR coefficient array (returned unchanged). PsiE Event shock impact matrix (returned unchanged). PsiR Regular shock impact matrix (returned unchanged). SigE Event shock variance (returned unchanged).
